name: Easy Run (Zone 1–2)
notes: Conversational pace; heart rate at or below aerobic threshold (AeT), typically <135 bpm or Maffetone (180 − age). Uphill Athlete: 80% of all aerobic volume at this intensity. Gym Jones recovery days: 60–120 min at conversational pace. Primary aerobic base builder. Nasal breathing is a useful intensity governor.

name: Tempo Run (Zone 3–4)
notes: Comfortably hard; can speak in short sentences. Near or at lactate threshold 2 (LT2) pace. CrossFit Endurance: threshold intervals 3–5 × 8 min at Zone 4. Uphill Athlete base-to-build phase transition increases this zone's proportion from 10% to 20% of weekly volume.

name: Interval Run (Zone 4–5)
notes: Gym Jones: 5× 1-min all-out, 4-min active recovery; or 4× 800 yards at 10% faster than 2-mile PR pace. CrossFit Endurance pyramid intervals: 1-2-3-4-3-2-1 min at Zone 3–4. Work:rest ratio typically 1:2 to 1:4.

name: Sprint Run (Max Effort)
notes: Flat-out maximal effort, ≤60 sec duration per rep. Gym Jones 3-mile time trial is the primary run performance test. Sprint work builds top-end speed and anaerobic capacity. Full warm-up mandatory; do not programme on days following high lower-body strength volume.

name: Easy Row (Zone 1–2)
notes: Concept II rower rated highest for metabolic conditioning by CrossFit Journal. Gym Jones recovery day format A: 60–120 min easy row at conversational pace. Damper setting 4–6 for most athletes. Stroke rate 18–22 SPM at recovery pace.

name: Threshold Row (Zone 3–4)
notes: Gym Jones: 6× 500m at ~15 sec per 500m slower than PR pace, 3-min active rest between pieces (1:2 work:rest). CrossFit Endurance threshold row format mirrors run threshold protocol. Stroke rate 24–28 SPM.

name: Interval Row (Zone 4–5)
notes: Gym Jones format: 15× (30-sec row / 90-sec rest), with target distance incrementing 1m per set (150m→165m). Alternatively: 4× 20-sec all-out / 10-sec rest (Tabata row). High CNS demand; limit to 2× per week.

name: 500m Row Time Trial
notes: Gym Jones benchmark: establish 500m PR pace; all interval training references this pace. CrossFit benchmark Jackie includes 1000m row. All-out effort; use as test 1–2× per training block, not as regular conditioning stimulus.

name: Easy Bike (Zone 1–2)
notes: Stationary or outdoor. Preferred aerobic rehabilitation modality when running is contraindicated (knee post-op, lower leg injuries). Uphill Athlete uses bike for aerobic base when impact work is unavailable. Minimal joint load; high aerobic adaptation per unit time.

name: Interval Bike (Zone 4–5)
notes: Tabata-protocol intervals or longer threshold intervals on bike. Assault bike commonly used for mixed-modal conditioning (e.g., Echo bike calories). Low injury risk relative to running intervals; useful when running is contraindicated.

name: Ruck (Loaded Walk / Run)
notes: Horsemen / SOF benchmark: Fan Dance standard = 22km, +1000m elevation, 30kg pack, 4 hours. Uphill Athlete aerobic ruck with 10–20% bodyweight. Gym Jones recovery endurance hike: 90–120 min with 20–30# pack. Progressions: distance first, then load, then speed. Simple, durable training modality requiring minimal equipment.

name: Easy Swim (Zone 1–2)
notes: Gym Jones recovery day format A includes swimming at conversational pace. Zero-impact; excellent when lower-limb injuries are present. Requires pool access. Technique-intensive; freestyle recommended for conditioning efficiency.

name: Ski Erg (Zone 1–2)
notes: Double-pole motion on Concept II SkiErg. Predominantly upper-body and core-driven aerobic demand; lower-limb contribution from hip hinge. Useful for aerobic base when running is contraindicated. Low stroke rate at Zone 1–2.

name: Ski Erg Interval (Zone 4–5)
notes: Tabata or 30/15-format intervals. High metabolic demand; upper-body fatigue accumulates quickly. Useful mixed-modal conditioning variation when rowing and running are both programmed in the same week.

name: Jump Rope (Single Under)
notes: Foundation jump rope skill. Light impact; builds rhythm and coordination prerequisite for double unders. CrossFit bodyweight WODs use single unders as the accessible scaling option. 1 double under = 3 single unders in most scaling schemes.

name: Jump Rope (Double Under)
notes: Rope passes twice per jump. CrossFit benchmark Annie: 50-40-30-20-10 double unders alternated with sit-ups. Requires rope speed, timing, and consistent jump height. Higher cardiovascular demand than single unders. Wrist mechanics and rope length are the primary limiting factors for beginners.

name: Back Squat
notes: Foundational lower-body strength movement. Horsemen standard: 2×BW squat. Starting Strength uses 3×5 linear progression as primary novice driver.

name: Front Squat
notes: Featured prominently in Gym Jones Operator programs (10×2 @ 1-min rest) and Horsemen strength cycles. Horsemen standard: 1.5×BW front squat. Requires wrist mobility and upright torso.

name: Overhead Squat
notes: Gym Jones uses OHS 5×3 as primary strength movement and tests 1RM. Demands significant shoulder mobility, thoracic extension, and ankle dorsiflexion. Referenced in Gym Jones SOTS press variant.

name: Box Squat
notes: Posterior-chain emphasis squat variant. Pausing on box removes stretch reflex and increases demand on hip extensors. Useful for athletes with poor squat depth mechanics or as powerlifting variation.

name: Deadlift
notes: Horsemen standard: 2.5×BW deadlift. Starting Strength programs 1×5 as novice primary. Gym Jones tests 1RM and builds training percentages from it (80% common). King of posterior-chain loading; prerequisite for all Olympic lifting derivatives.

name: Romanian Deadlift
notes: Gym Jones programs SLDL (stiff-leg DL / RDL) at 95–135# for 3×10 in Accumulation sessions. Emphasizes hamstring length and posterior-chain resilience. Hip hingeing with a neutral spine under load; bar remains close to legs.

name: Sumo Deadlift
notes: Wide-stance deadlift variant shifting more demand to hip abductors and quads. Shorter range of motion than conventional; suits athletes with longer torsos or limited hip mobility.

name: Deficit Deadlift
notes: Performed standing on plates or blocks (2–4 inch deficit). Increases range of motion demanding greater hip and hamstring recruitment at the bottom. Builds starting strength off the floor.

name: Bench Press
notes: Horsemen standard: 1.5×BW bench press. Starting Strength uses 3×5 linear progression as novice primary horizontal pressing movement.

name: Floor Press
notes: Pressing from the floor eliminates the leg drive and stretch reflex from bench pressing. Used in Gym Jones programming; limits range of motion which can be shoulder-friendly for some presentations. Removes lower-body contribution.

name: Close-Grip Bench Press
notes: Grip inside shoulder width, elbows tucked. Shifts demand toward triceps and medial deltoid. Used as tricep-dominant pressing accessory. Grip narrower than shoulder-width but not so narrow as to cause wrist deviation.

name: Strict Press (Overhead)
notes: Horsemen standard: 1×BW military press. Starting Strength uses 3×5 linear progression. No leg drive permitted; pure upper-body pressing strength. Foundational prerequisite for all jerk and push-press variants.

name: Push Press
notes: Hip dip and drive transfers leg power into the bar; arms lockout overhead. Used in Gym Jones Six-Way Barbell Complex and Horsemen power-endurance workouts (e.g. 5 push press while holding 45# bar for 400m).

name: Push Jerk
notes: Drive bar overhead then dip under into quarter squat to catch. Horsemen Olympic Day features jerk in wave patterns (3r/2r/1r) at 80–90%. Tests 1RM in Horsemen power benchmarks.

name: Split Jerk
notes: Drive bar overhead and split legs into lunge position to receive. Most technically demanding jerk variant. Bilateral is false because each repetition uses an asymmetric split stance. Horsemen tests 1RM split jerk.

name: Barbell Row (Bent Over)
notes: Featured in the Gym Jones / Horsemen Six-Way Barbell Complex (6 reps). Starting Strength uses as the primary horizontal pull for novice programming. Hip-hinged torso roughly parallel to floor; bar pulled into lower chest.

name: Pendlay Row
notes: Bar returns to floor each rep (dead-stop). Torso parallel to floor, explosive pull. Removes momentum from barbell row and demands greater power per rep. Named for Glenn Pendlay.

name: Power Clean
notes: Starting Strength programs 1×5 power clean as the primary upper-back and power movement on alternate days. Gym Jones references clean triplets (EMOM 10 min). Horsemen Olympic Day programs cleans in wave patterns at 80–90%.

name: Hang Power Clean
notes: Pull initiates from hang position (above knee). Removes the first pull from the floor, emphasizing the hip snap and turnover. Featured in Horsemen "Purple Church" EMOM (3 power clean + 3 hang squat clean + 3 push press).

name: Squat Clean
notes: Full clean receiving the bar in a full squat. Highest expression of the clean movement. Demands front rack mobility, squat depth, and fast footwork. Horsemen Olympic Day programs at 80–90% in wave patterns.

name: Hang Squat Clean
notes: Horsemen "Purple Church" EMOM uses hang squat clean in a triplet sequence. Combines the limited range-of-motion pull with a full receiving squat. Highly metabolic; used in conditioning-strength hybrid sessions.

name: Power Snatch
notes: Bar pulled from floor to overhead lockout, receiving above parallel. Horsemen Olympic Day ("Snatch Your Face") programs snatch at 80–90% in waves. Prerequisite to full snatch; builds the hip-to-overhead pathway.

name: Hang Power Snatch
notes: Initiates from hang position above the knee. Removes the floor pull and isolates the second pull and turnover. Useful for coaching overhead position and hip extension timing without the complexity of the full first pull.

name: Snatch
notes: Full Olympic snatch: bar from floor to overhead in one movement, receiving in a full squat. Most technically demanding barbell movement. Horsemen programs at 80–90% in wave patterns. Tests 1RM for power benchmarks.

name: Good Morning
notes: Bar on upper traps, hinge forward until torso is roughly parallel to floor, return to standing. Develops hamstring and spinal erector strength in a lengthened position. Starting Strength programs as posterior-chain accessory. Moderate loads for high reps; not a max-effort movement.

name: Barbell Complex (Six-Way)
notes: Six-movement barbell warm-up complex used by both Gym Jones and Horsemen: Deadlift → Bent Row → Hang Clean → Front Squat → Push Press → Back Squat (6 reps each, no rest between movements, bar does not touch the floor between the row and clean). Performed for 3–4 sets with progressive loading: 75#–85#–95#–105#. 1–2 min rest between sets. Serves as both warm-up and conditioning stimulus.

name: Thruster
notes: Front squat into push press in a single continuous movement. The squat drives the bar off the shoulders; the hips extend and press the bar overhead in one motion. Featured in CrossFit benchmark "Fran" (21-15-9 thrusters + pull-ups) and Horsemen breathing ladder format. One of the most metabolically demanding barbell movements.

name: Push-Up
notes: Foundation horizontal push. Cue: full lockout at top, chest to floor at bottom, elbows at 45 degrees. Scale up via ring, archer, or clapping variants. Gym Jones programs 200+ reps in Murph-style sessions.

name: Push-Up (Rings)
notes: Rings demand active shoulder stabilization throughout the press. Turn rings out at lockout (RTO). Significant recruitment of pec minor, serratus anterior, and rotator cuff relative to bar push-up.

name: Push-Up (Clapping)
notes: Plyometric push-up variant. Emphasises rate of force development in the horizontal push. Cue: explosive concentric to generate airtime; controlled landing. Do not programme when fatigued — wrist/shoulder injury risk.

name: Push-Up (Archer)
notes: Unilateral loading progression toward one-arm push-up. Wide stance; working arm bends fully, assisting arm extends laterally with minimal weight-bearing. Each side trained independently.

name: Pull-Up (Overhand)
notes: Pronated grip, full hang to chin over bar. Gym Jones programs pull-up ladders (1-2-3-4-5-6 per set) accumulating 50+ reps. CrossFit benchmark Murph: 100 reps. Prerequisite dead_hang ensures shoulder stability under passive load before active pulling.

name: Chin-Up (Supinated)
notes: Supinated grip increases biceps contribution; generally accessible for beginners due to stronger elbow flexor line. Useful substitute when pronated pull-up volume is high to reduce elbow fatigue.

name: Pull-Up (Weighted)
notes: Belt, vest, or dumbbell between knees. Primary strength developer for vertical pull. Gym Jones references adding load as the primary progression for the pull (reduce rest periods secondary).

name: Pull-Up (Rings)
notes: Rings allow natural rotation of the forearm throughout the pull. Higher stabiliser demand than bar. Turn rings out at the top for full shoulder pack. Prerequisite for ring muscle-up.

name: Dip (Bar / Parallel Bars)
notes: Shoulder-width grip on bars or parallel bars. Slight forward lean loads chest; upright torso emphasises triceps. Full range: shoulder below elbow at bottom. CrossFit benchmark Elizabeth uses ring dips.

name: Dip (Rings)
notes: Turn rings out (RTO) at lockout. Greater pectoralis minor and serratus demand than bar dip. CrossFit benchmark Elizabeth programs ring dips as a primary movement.

name: Handstand Hold (Wall)
notes: Chest-to-wall or back-to-wall hold. Active shoulders — push floor away, protract scapulae. Foundation for HSPU. Ido Portal treats inversion practice as a daily movement habit.

name: Handstand Push-Up (Wall)
notes: Chest-to-wall kick-up. Head to floor at bottom; full lockout at top. Deficit HSPU (hands on parallettes) increases range of motion and difficulty. Kipping variant increases volume capacity for metabolic conditioning; strict variant is primary strength developer.

name: Handstand Push-Up (Rings)
notes: Extreme stability demand. Rings set low; athlete kicks to inverted and controls descent onto rings. Requires total shoulder stability and considerable proprioception. Ido Portal advanced ring work.

name: Muscle-Up (Bar)
notes: Explosive pull through and above bar transitioning to dip lockout. Strict variant demands significant lat, biceps, and triceps power. High injury risk if kipping without adequate strict strength base. Do not programme with active shoulder_instability flag.

name: Muscle-Up (Rings)
notes: Transition from below rings to support position in a single movement. The false grip enables a smooth pull-to-press transition. CrossFit benchmark movement. Higher shoulder demand than bar variant due to free rotation.

name: Plank Hold (FLR — Front Leaning Rest)
notes: Gym Jones programs 300-second accumulated FLR (Front Leaning Rest) as a core and shoulder endurance component. Maintain neutral spine; do not allow hip sag. Progress toward 5 continuous minutes before loading.

name: Side Plank Hold
notes: Lateral core anti-lateral flexion demand. Elbow or hand variant. Used in Marcus Filly shoulder rehab protocols for lat activation. Train each side independently.

name: Sit-Up (Abmat)
notes: CrossFit benchmark Annie: 50-40-30-20-10 sit-ups. Gym Jones uses in accumulation days as high-rep core work. Anchored foot variant increases hip flexor contribution; abmat allows full lumbar extension at bottom.

name: L-Sit (Parallettes or Floor)
notes: Full hip flexion hold with straight legs, locked elbows, depressed scapulae. Accumulate time in hold; 60 sec total volume is a reasonable benchmark. Ido Portal uses as baseline compression strength indicator.

name: Tuck Sit (Parallettes or Floor)
notes: Knees tucked to chest in supported hold. Regression of L-sit. Develops scapular depression strength and hip flexor compression. Ido Portal prescribes as a daily practice movement.

name: Air Squat
notes: CrossFit fundamental movement. Rippetoe squatting standards apply: below parallel, neutral spine, heels flat. Gym Jones warm-up protocol: 2×5 wall squats then 2×10 air squats before loading. Chelsea benchmark: 15 squats per minute EMOM 30 min.

name: Jump Squat
notes: Squat to full depth then explode vertically. Land soft, reabsorb through hip/knee/ankle. Gym Jones programs tuck jumps and split jumps as variants. High-impact; contraindicated post knee surgery.

name: Pistol Squat (Single-Leg Squat)
notes: Single-leg squat to full depth with opposite leg extended forward. High demand on hip flexor strength and ankle dorsiflexion mobility. Gym Jones single-leg squat on 18–20" box is the primary regression.

name: Walking Lunge
notes: Alternating legs, stepping forward. Knee tracks over foot, front shin near-vertical. Gym Jones programs 2×20m walking lunges in single-leg accessory work. Bodyweight version is the baseline; progress to overhead or weighted variants.

name: Split Jump Lunge
notes: Plyometric lunge: explosive vertical jump from split stance, switch legs mid-air. High metabolic and power demand. Gym Jones references split jumps in lower body power circuits.

name: Overhead Lunge
notes: Arms locked overhead with weight or simply arms raised. Gymn Jones programs 45lb bar overhead during 400m lunge walks as a power-endurance challenge. Without load this is a mobility and stability developer.

name: Burpee
notes: Gym Jones accumulation day benchmark: 60 burpees as final element of "Those Burpees Suck" circuit. High metabolic cost, full-body demand. Standard: chest-to-floor, jump-and-clap overhead at top.

name: Dead Hang
notes: Passive hang, shoulders fully elevated (shrug into ears) or active (scapular depression). Gym Jones programs 300-second accumulated dead hang on accumulation days. Decompresses spine; builds grip and shoulder passive stability prerequisite for pulling movements.

name: Rope Climb
notes: 15-foot standard height (CrossFit). Legless variant eliminates lower-body assistance and dramatically increases upper-body demand. Rope climb scores high on lat/biceps activation and grip endurance. Foot-assisted technique for conditioning; legless for strength.

name: Box Jump
notes: Standard CrossFit box: 24" male, 20" female. Gym Jones uses 20–24" boxes in circuits (e.g., 30 box jumps in "Those Burpees Suck"). Land softly in partial squat; step down (do not jump down) to protect Achilles under fatigue. Jump-and-land variant vs. rebound variant — program intentionally.

name: Man Maker (Bodyweight / Dumbbell)
notes: With DBs: plank on DBs → push-up → renegade row (each arm) → stand up into squat → press. Each rep is a compound sequence. Gym Jones programs DB complexes incorporating this movement pattern. Scale load by reducing DB weight; bodyweight version removes push-up row but retains squat + press.

name: Farmer Carry (Barbell)
notes: Barbell held at thigh in each hand (trap-bar if available) or one barbell in each hand. Higher load ceiling than KB or DB variant; useful for max-load carries. Maintain neutral spine; no lateral lean. Gym Jones programs farmer carry as a loaded carry accessory.

name: Farmer Carry (Kettlebell)
notes: Primary farmer carry variation. One KB in each hand, arms straight, packed shoulders. High grip, trapezius, and core anti-lateral-flexion demand. Horsemen and SOF-style GPP staple. Unlocks rack and overhead carry once positional stability is established.

name: Farmer Carry (Dumbbell)
notes: Entry-level farmer carry using dumbbells. Lower load ceiling than KB or barbell but accessible with standard gym equipment. Technique and breathing mechanics same as KB variant. Gym Jones uses DB carries in circuit and accumulation days.

name: Rack Carry (Kettlebell)
notes: KB(s) held in rack position (bell resting on forearm, elbow tucked, wrist neutral). Core bracing and vertical posture are the primary demands. Can be done unilaterally (one bell) or bilaterally. Unilateral rack carry is a strong anti-lateral-flexion challenge.

name: Rack Carry (Barbell — Front Rack)
notes: Barbell in front-rack position (fingertips on bar, elbows high) while walking. High thoracic mobility and wrist mobility demand. Used in Horsemen-style loaded carry circuits. Heavier loading ceiling than KB variant.

name: Overhead Carry (Kettlebell)
notes: KB locked out overhead, arm fully extended, shoulder packed (active upward rotation). Typically performed unilaterally; bilateral variant possible with two bells. High rotator cuff and serratus anterior demand. Walk tall; do not side-bend under load. Train each arm independently.

name: Overhead Carry (Barbell)
notes: Barbell locked out overhead while walking. Gym Jones programs overhead hold static (300-sec accumulated) as a core/shoulder endurance component; overhead carry is the ambulatory progression. High scapular and rotator cuff demand. Requires solid overhead squat mechanics before loading.

name: Suitcase Carry (Unilateral)
notes: Single implement (KB, DB, or heavy object) carried on one side while maintaining a vertical torso. Primary anti-lateral-flexion demand on the contralateral oblique/quadratus lumborum. Gym Jones references suitcase carry in loaded carry accessory work. Alternate sides each set.

name: Zercher Carry (Barbell)
notes: Barbell held in the crook of the elbows, forearms crossed. Chest-level load keeps the centre of mass high and forward. High anterior core demand; upper-back and biceps fatigue is a limiting factor. Horsemen GPP carries this as a sandbag or barbell variation.

name: Yoke Carry
notes: A-frame yoke loaded with plates; athlete walks under the yoke with the crossbar across the upper back. Highest absolute load potential in the carry family. Strong-man derivative used in Horsemen GPP for total-body structural durability and compressive loading.

name: Sandbag Shoulder Carry
notes: Sandbag draped over one shoulder, load sitting on the upper trapezius. Horsemen staple for GPP and durability work. Unilateral; alternate shoulders each set or at midpoint. The uneven, shifting load of the sandbag demands continuous stabilisation that rigid implements do not.

name: Sandbag Bear-Hug Carry
notes: Sandbag hugged against the chest with both arms. High anterior core and thoracic erector demand. Breathing is compressed; heavy bags become a respiratory challenge as well as a strength-endurance test. Horsemen GPP primary sandbag carry.

name: Ruck Carry (Loaded — Strength Focus)
notes: Slow, heavy, deliberate loaded carry as a strength-endurance exercise, distinct from the aerobic ruck (which is higher volume, lower load, time-based). Pack weight ≥30% bodyweight; pace is controlled walk. Horsemen Fan Dance standard: 30kg, 22km with +1000m elevation. This entry targets shorter heavy training sets rather than aerobic events.

name: Tire Drag (Forward)
notes: Harness or rope attached to tire; athlete walks or runs forward dragging load. High posterior chain, hip, and grip demand. Horsemen GPP staple. Impact-free locomotion pattern useful when running volume is high. Tyre weight adjusted by adding plates inside.

name: Tire Drag (Backward)
notes: Backward walking with tire in tow. ATG / Knees Over Toes principle: backward sled and tire drag loads the VMO and quad through terminal extension range without compressive load on the knee. Used in Horsemen GPP and as a knee prehab modality. Primary quad-focused carry variant.

name: Sled Push
notes: Low (handles near ground, body angled forward) or upright (handles at chest height) variants. High-load sled push develops strength-endurance and posterior chain drive; lighter fast sled push develops anaerobic power. No eccentric component — extremely low DOMS; can be programmed frequently. Horsemen GPP uses sled push as a primary conditioning tool.

name: Wall Squat (Mobility Drill)
notes: Gym Jones warm-up staple used in virtually every strength and circuit session. Stand with toes touching a wall, feet wide. Drop into a squat with knees tracking toes; contact the wall with knees to enforce vertical shin position and upright torso. Hands brace against wall as needed. Builds ankle mobility, hip flexor opening, and thoracic extension. Not a strength exercise — a diagnostic and mobility drill. 2–3 sets of 5 before any lower body session.

name: Single-Leg Squat (Box)
notes: Standing on a 18–20 inch box, lower on one leg until the non-working heel touches the ground, then stand. Uses the box to limit depth and provide a proprioceptive endpoint — more accessible than a full pistol squat. Gym Jones Operator Fitness I and II primary single-leg strength exercise, performed 5×5. Eliminates assistance from the trailing leg. Builds posterior chain, quad, and hip stability for unilateral movement demands. Progress by lowering the box height toward full pistol range.

name: Weighted Step-Up
notes: Barbell on back (or DBs at side), step up onto a 20-inch box. Eliminate all assistance from the trailing leg — this is a true single-leg lift. Gym Jones Operator Fitness prescription: work up to heavy single (95–135 lb) then perform 5×2 each side at that load, rest 60 sec between sets. Higher transfer to unilateral field demands (stairs, slopes, climbing) than bilateral squat alone. Emphasis on single-leg deployment fitness.

name: Bear Crawl
notes: Quadruped locomotion — hips close to the ground, opposite limbs moving simultaneously. Gym Jones uses bear crawl in conditioning circuits and as a loaded carry accessory (DB bear crawl: hands on dumbbells, hop feet forward). Builds shoulder stability, hip flexor endurance, and total-body coordination. Weighted version: push DBs forward, hop feet up in plank position. Distance-based or time-based.

name: Ring Support Hold (FLR on Rings)
notes: Arms straight, rings turned out at bottom of dip position, body held in a tight plank. The ring instability dramatically increases rotator cuff and serratus anterior demand vs. fixed bar support. Gym Jones uses "300 seconds of FLR (Front Leaning Rest)" accumulated across sets as a core and shoulder endurance block. Target: 60 seconds per set × 5 sets. Turn rings out progressively to increase scapular stability demand.

name: KB Swing Breathing Ladder (1-20-1)
notes: Gym Jones breathing ladder protocol: perform 1 KB swing, take 1 breath, perform 2 swings, take 2 breaths... climb to 20, then descend back to 1. Total: ~400 swings. The breathing is the rest — breath count equals rep count, enforcing complete oxygen recovery between sets. KB weight: 24 kg (53 lb) for trained athletes. The breathing ladder develops extraordinary KB endurance and trains the ability to recover during work. Mark Twight: "The breathing ladder is a test of will as much as fitness." Warm up with the same bell at lower rep counts before starting the full ladder.

name: Kettlebell Swing (Two-Hand)
notes: Foundational kettlebell ballistic. Hip-hinge driven; bell floats to shoulder height on the backswing hip snap. Horsemen warm-up complex: 1 min two-hand swings. Gym Jones breathing ladder uses swings 1–20 at 53#. Wildman progression begins here. Two hands on the bell; posterior-chain power driver.

name: Kettlebell Swing (Single-Arm)
notes: One hand on the bell; free hand assists with balance and counter-rotation. Horsemen warm-up complex: 1 min swings each arm (left then right). Direct prerequisite to single-arm clean and snatch. Anti-rotation demand is greater than two-hand swing.

name: Kettlebell Clean (Single-Arm)
notes: Bell pulled from swing arc into rack position at shoulder. Elbow nestled against body; bell resting on forearm. Horsemen 12-min KB complex: 1 min clean & press each arm. Wildman progression: swing → clean → press → snatch.

name: Kettlebell Clean (Double)
notes: Two bells simultaneously cleaned to double rack position. Bilateral load significantly increases systemic demand. Prerequisite for all double-bell pressing and squatting patterns. Featured in kettlebell workout AMRAP cycles.

name: Kettlebell Press (Single-Arm)
notes: Strict shoulder press from rack position. No leg drive. Horsemen 12-min warm-up complex uses clean & press 1 min each arm. Kettlebell workout cycles feature press in all three cycles. Requires tight lat engagement at lockout.

name: Kettlebell Press (Double)
notes: Both bells pressed from double rack simultaneously. Significantly higher systemic demand and core bracing requirement than single press. Cycle 1 in kettlebell workout schedule prominently features double KB push press and press at 2×16kg.

name: Kettlebell Push Press (Single-Arm)
notes: Hip dip drives bell off shoulder; arm locks out overhead. Horsemen warm-up protocol: 1 min push press each arm. KB workout standard warm-up cycle includes push press as timed interval. Bridge movement from strict press to jerk.

name: Kettlebell Jerk (Single-Arm)
notes: Double dip jerk: first dip drives bell overhead, second dip catches arm locked under bell. KB sport RPM target: 10 RPM at 1 pood (16kg). KB workout timed protocol: 8 min at 10 RPM, switching hands every 2 min. Primary KB pentathlon movement; 120 reps in 6 min (20 RPM) is the pentathlon standard.

name: Kettlebell Jerk (Double)
notes: Two bells jerked simultaneously from double rack. Highest systemic demand of all KB pressing patterns. Demands exceptional breathing mechanics and core stability. KB pentathlon long cycle clean & jerk uses double bells.

name: Kettlebell Snatch
notes: Bell punched from hip-hinge to overhead lockout in one movement. Horsemen 12-min complex: 1 min snatch each arm. Horsemen SSST standard: 200+ snatches in 10 min at 53# (Team Guy standard). KB pentathlon half-snatch: 108 reps in 6 min. KB sport RPM target: 14 RPM at 1 pood.

name: Kettlebell Goblet Squat
notes: Bell held at chest level with both hands, elbows track inside knees. Gym Jones uses goblet squat (25#) in strength day warm-up: 2×5. Excellent teaching tool for squat mechanics. Low technical demand; appropriate as first-session squat pattern.

name: Kettlebell Double Front Squat
notes: Two bells held in rack position; front squat pattern. Anterior load demands significant thoracic extension and core bracing. More demanding than goblet squat; requires double clean to set up.

name: Kettlebell Turkish Get-Up
notes: From lying to standing with bell locked overhead throughout; reverse to return. Foundational Wildman movement. Gym Jones programs 100 TGUs at 15# (recovery day) and 5–6 reps at 25# (strength day core work with 60-sec FLR). Horsemen SMMF: 100 get-ups at 35# KB (15–20 min). Unlocks all overhead carries. Tests shoulder stability, hip mobility, and whole-body integration.

name: Kettlebell Deadlift
notes: Hip-hinge pattern with kettlebell between feet, pulled to standing. No prerequisites beyond basic motor control; appropriate as the first movement taught. Scaling-down option for barbell deadlift. Direct gateway to swings; teaches the hip hinge without ballistic demand.

name: Kettlebell Step-Up (Single-Arm)
notes: Single-arm kettlebell step-up. Hold one KB at the chest (rack) or goblet position. Drive through the heel of the lead foot; no assistance from the trailing leg. 5×2 each side at a heavy bell. KB step-up approximates the Gym Jones loaded step-up protocol when a barbell is unavailable. Box height governs difficulty — a higher box increases unilateral demand.

name: Kettlebell Row (Single-Arm)
notes: Hip-hinged torso; opposite hand braced on knee or bench. Bell pulled to hip. Gym Jones programs one-arm row in circuit days. Anti-rotation demand develops lateral core stability absent from bilateral barbell rows.

name: Kettlebell Figure-Eight
notes: Bell passed between legs in figure-eight pattern, alternating hands. Horsemen 12-min warm-up complex includes figure-eight both directions. Develops rotational hip mobility, lateral hip hinge control, and hand-switching timing.

name: Kettlebell Halo
notes: Bell circled around the head in a halo pattern, both directions. Horsemen 12-min complex includes slasher-to-halo. Develops thoracic and shoulder mobility; prepares the shoulder girdle for overhead loading. Appropriate as first-session shoulder prep.

name: Kettlebell Windmill
notes: Bell locked overhead; athlete hinges laterally to touch floor with free hand, maintaining overhead lockout. Develops lateral hip mobility, shoulder stability under rotation, and hamstring length. One of the most demanding single-arm overhead stability exercises.

name: Kettlebell Farmer Carry (Single)
notes: Single kettlebell carried in one hand at side for distance or time. Asymmetric load demands lateral core stability. Gym Jones programs farmer carry in loaded carry circuits. Foundation for all loaded carry variants.

name: Kettlebell Farmer Carry (Double)
notes: Two kettlebells carried bilaterally for distance or time. Featured in AMRAP kettlebell workouts (20m farmer's walk between sets). Develops grip endurance, trapezius, and lateral core under bilateral load.

name: Kettlebell Suitcase Carry
notes: Identical to farmer carry but emphasizes anti-lateral-flexion: the athlete resists the bell pulling the torso sideways. Gym Jones programs suitcase carry in loaded carry circuits. Trains the quadratus lumborum and lateral core in a functional walking pattern.

name: Kettlebell Rack Carry
notes: Bell held in rack position at shoulder while walking. Anti-flexion and anti-rotation demand on the ipsilateral hip and core. Horsemen progressive TGU protocol feeds into rack carry as shoulder stability development. Intermediate between farmer carry and overhead carry.

name: Kettlebell Overhead Carry
notes: Bell pressed and held at full overhead lockout while walking. Maximum shoulder stability demand of all carry variants. Scapular upward rotation and packed shoulder essential. Horsemen uses overhead carries in loaded carry circuits. Requires intact shoulder function; contraindicated with most shoulder pathologies.

name: Kettlebell 12-Minute Complex (Horsemen Warm-Up)
notes: Official Horsemen 12-minute kettlebell warm-up complex at 35# (16kg). Sequence (1 minute each, no rest): two-hand swings, left-arm swings, right-arm swings, figure-eight both directions, clean & press left, clean & press right, slasher-to-halo ×2, snatch left, snatch right, lunge with KB left, lunge with KB right. Total: 12 movements, 12 minutes, continuous. Used as warm-up before strength or conditioning sessions.

name: Hip Circle
notes: Standing hip circumduction — large circles in both directions. Standard joint prep for any lower body session. Low threshold entry movement for hip mobility warm-up sequences.

name: Hip 90/90 Stretch
notes: Seated with both hips at 90 degrees — one internally rotated, one externally rotated. Addresses both internal and external hip rotation. Transition between sides is itself a mobility drill. Hold or apply gentle oscillating pressure.

name: Hip Flexor Stretch (kneeling)
notes: Half-kneeling lunge position, drive hips forward to load iliopsoas. Posterior pelvic tilt cue to deepen stretch. Essential counter to prolonged sitting and heavy squat/deadlift days.

name: Pigeon Pose
notes: External hip rotation stretch targeting piriformis and external rotators. Can be performed supine (figure-4) for those with knee discomfort. Contraindicated with acute meniscus injury due to knee flexion under hip rotation load.

name: Ankle Circle
notes: Non-weight-bearing ankle circumduction. Standard joint prep before any lower body or locomotive work. Perform both clockwise and counterclockwise.

name: Ankle Calf Stretch (gastrocnemius)
notes: Straight-leg calf stretch targeting gastrocnemius. Wall-lean or step-edge variant. Address dorsiflexion restriction before squat depth work.

name: Soleus Stretch (bent-knee calf)
notes: Bent-knee ankle flexion stretch isolating soleus. Critical for deep squat dorsiflexion. More directly relevant to squat mechanics than straight-leg variant.

name: Thoracic Rotation (quadruped or seated)
notes: Quadruped open-book rotation or seated thoracic rotation. Targets mid-back rotational mobility. Prerequisite context for overhead work and rotational patterns. Perform slowly with exhale at end range.

name: Thoracic Extension (foam roll)
notes: Foam roller placed perpendicular to spine at mid-back. Segment-by-segment extension over roller. Avoid rolling lumbar spine directly. Contraindicated with active lumbar disc pathology due to spinal extension load.

name: Shoulder Circle
notes: Standing arm circles in both directions at varied speeds. Standard shoulder joint prep before any upper body or overhead work. Both forward and backward directions required.

name: Shoulder Dislocate (PVC pipe)
notes: Wide-grip PVC pass from front to overhead to behind back and return. Progressively narrow grip as shoulder mobility improves. Contraindicated with shoulder instability (hypermobility) or wrist pathology. Use resistance band as regression if PVC feels binding.

name: Band Pull-Apart
notes: Horizontal band pull-apart at chest height or overhead. Activates posterior deltoid, infraspinatus, and lower trapezius. Critical for shoulder health maintenance. Prehab staple for any pressing-heavy program.

name: Face Pull (band or cable)
notes: Pull toward face with thumbs-up grip, elbows high and flared. Full external rotation at end range. Targets rotator cuff, posterior deltoid, and lower/mid trapezius. High-frequency exercise; can be performed daily as prehab without recovery cost.

name: Wrist Circle
notes: Wrist circumduction in both directions. Part of Ido Portal wrist prep routine. Perform before any load-bearing hand position (push-ups, handstands, kettlebell work).

name: Wrist Flexion Stretch
notes: Extend arm forward, pull fingers back toward body to stretch wrist flexors. Part of Ido Portal wrist routine. Contraindicated with acute wrist injury.

name: Wrist Extension Stretch
notes: Point fingers down and press back of hand away to stretch wrist extensors. Counterbalances flexion stretch. Use floor press (quadruped wrist mobility) as loaded variant.

name: Ido Portal Squat Clinic Routine
notes: Ido Portal's standardized squat mobility sequence — includes deep squat hold, weight shift, heel raises, internal/external rotation exploration, and ankle mobilization. Performed as one continuous set at session start. Duration 3-5 min. Part of the standard Floreio warm-up.

name: Scapula Mobilization
notes: Scapular protraction/retraction, depression/elevation, and circumduction. Listed in Ido Portal's cool-down (3 sets, 60-sec rest). Addresses scapulohumeral rhythm restrictions. Perform as cool-down staple after any overhead or pushing session.

name: Couch Stretch (hip flexor/quad)
notes: Rear foot elevated against wall, knee on floor, hip driven toward wall. Targets rectus femoris and hip flexor complex simultaneously. One of the most effective hip extension mobility drills. Contraindicated with acute knee conditions due to end-range knee flexion load.

name: World Greatest Stretch
notes: Lunge forward, drop elbow to floor inside front foot, rotate arm overhead — combined hip flexor, thoracic rotation, and ankle mobility in one movement. Standard pre-session prep. Performed slowly with 3-sec pause at each position for maximum benefit.

name: Neck Rotation
notes: Slow, controlled cervical rotation to each side. Standard joint prep. No forceful range of motion. Contraindicated with active cervical spine injury.

name: Neck Lateral Flexion
notes: Slow ear-to-shoulder lateral neck flexion. Targets scalenes and lateral cervical musculature. Complements neck rotation in cervical joint prep sequence. Contraindicated with active cervical spine injury.

name: Quad Set (Isometric Quad Contraction)
notes: Early-phase knee rehab. Lying flat — roll towel under knee, contract quad to press knee down, hold 5-10 sec. No joint movement. Programs within days of surgery or acute injury. Does not require cleared ROM. Activate VMO focus.

name: Straight Leg Raise (SLR)
notes: Supine — contract quad fully on affected leg, keep knee locked, lift to 45 degrees and hold 2 sec. Early phase knee rehab. Progresses to ankle weight (0.5kg increments). Program bilateral to maintain quad symmetry on unaffected side.

name: Heel Slide
notes: Supine — slide heel toward glute to increase knee flexion ROM, hold at max range 5 sec, return. Early phase knee rehab for restoring flexion ROM. Active or assisted. Track ROM progress toward 90 degrees then full range milestones.

name: Ankle Pump
notes: Immediate post-op or injury — repeated ankle dorsiflexion/plantarflexion while leg is elevated. Promotes venous return and reduces swelling. Can be performed in bed or chair. High frequency (every 1-2 hours) in acute phase. No contraindications.

name: Step-Up Low (Rehab, 4-6 inch)
notes: Rehab step-up on 4-6 inch platform only. Controlled eccentric down. Focus on single-leg control and avoiding knee valgus. Mid-phase knee rehab milestone. Progression to higher box only when 10 reps pain-free at current height. Use bodyweight only — no added load.

name: Terminal Knee Extension (Band)
notes: Band looped behind knee, standing. Drive knee to full extension against band resistance. Targets VMO and terminal knee extension mechanics — critical for patellofemoral function and post-op quad recruitment. ATG-style application. Program early to mid phase.

name: Single-Leg Press (Light, Rehab)
notes: Machine leg press — single leg, very light load, controlled ROM. Mid-phase knee rehab for tissue loading with joint protection. Progress ROM from partial to full as tolerated. Bilateral press as stepping-stone if single-leg is too demanding. Note in programming: requires active knee rehab flag.

name: Bulgarian Split Squat (Light, Rehab)
notes: Rear foot elevated split squat, bodyweight only initially. Mid-phase knee rehab. Controls single-leg depth while rear foot provides stability assist. Progress load only when pain-free through full ROM. Note: requires active knee rehab flag to program.

name: Single-Leg Deadlift (Light, Rehab)
notes: Single-leg Romanian deadlift with bodyweight or very light load. Mid-phase knee rehab — builds posterior chain without high knee compression. Emphasizes hip hinge and proprioceptive knee control. Requires active knee rehab flag to program.

name: Stationary Bike (Easy, Rehab)
notes: Low-resistance cycling as rehab modality. Use when sufficient knee flexion ROM restored (typically 90+ degrees). No-impact cardiovascular maintenance during knee rehab phase. Progress from 10 min to 20-30 min. Seat height set high initially to minimize flexion demand.

name: DB External Rotation (Elbow on Knee)
notes: Seated — elbow resting on knee, forearm hanging. Rotate forearm upward against light dumbbell resistance. Targets infraspinatus and teres minor. Cornerstone rotator cuff rehab exercise. Start with 1-2 kg. Slow controlled tempo — 3 sec up, 3 sec down.

name: Band Pull-Apart (Rehab variant)
notes: Light resistance band horizontal pull-apart at chest height. Shoulder rehab variant — use lightest band available initially. Multiple directions: horizontal, diagonal, overhead. High frequency safe — can program daily. Addresses posterior deltoid and lower trapezius weakness contributing to impingement.

name: Face Pull (Rehab variant)
notes: Rehab-weight face pull — thumbs-up grip, elbows high, full external rotation at end range. Used in shoulder impingement and instability protocols per Marcus Filly approach. Cue external rotation at end range — do not let elbows drop. Contraindication-free; safe in virtually all shoulder pathologies.

name: Landmine Press
notes: Single-arm press into barbell fixed in corner at angle. Path is angled rather than vertical — reduces subacromial impingement compared to overhead press. Shoulder rehab pushing pattern per Marcus Filly protocol. No contraindications in typical shoulder rehab scenarios.

name: Eccentric Push-Up (Rehab, on parallettes)
notes: Push-up on parallettes with slow 5-sec eccentric (lowering phase) and assisted or step-up concentric. Reduces wrist deviation compared to floor push-up. Shoulder rehab pushing pattern — eccentric loading for tendon remodeling. Marcus Filly protocol. Parallettes allow greater ROM and neutral wrist position.

name: Dip Support Hold (Isometric)
notes: Supported on dip bars or parallettes, arms locked, hold isometric top position. Shoulder rehab isometric per Marcus Filly — builds scapular depression and lat engagement without joint movement. Cue scapular depression throughout hold. Progress duration before adding dynamic dip range.

name: Side Plank with Lat Activation
notes: Standard side plank with deliberate lat/serratus activation — cue pulling shoulder blade down and away from ear. Shoulder rehabilitation isometric. Programs alongside dip support hold in shoulder protocol. Addresses serratus anterior weakness common in impingement presentations.

name: Turkish Get-Up (Rehab weight)
notes: TGU with very light KB (4-8 kg) as progressive carry and shoulder stability rehab. Marcus Filly progressive carry protocol. Entire TGU movement pattern challenges shoulder in multiple planes through full ROM — ideal late-stage shoulder rehab. Progress load incrementally once full pain-free TGU is achieved.

name: Dead Bug
notes: Supine — opposite arm and leg extend slowly while lumbar spine presses into floor. Core anti-extension rehab. Lumbar spine position — not neutral — pressed flat throughout. Foundational spinal stability exercise for any back, hip, or knee rehab phase.

name: Bird Dog
notes: Quadruped — extend opposite arm and leg simultaneously, hold 3-5 sec, return. Core stability and anti-rotation. Follows dead bug in rehab progressions. Essential for lumbar, hip, and shoulder rehab as stabilizer recruitment baseline.

name: Glute Bridge
notes: Supine, feet flat, drive hips to full extension and hold 2 sec. Bilateral glute activation for knee and hip rehab. Targets glute max and posterior chain without knee loading. Programs in all lower extremity rehab phases — often combined with quad set early phase.

name: Single-Leg Glute Bridge
notes: Single-leg glute bridge — non-working leg either extended in air or crossed over. Identifies and addresses bilateral glute strength asymmetry. Mid-phase rehab progression from bilateral glute bridge. Excellent for hip and knee rehab without significant joint load.

name: Nordic Hamstring Curl (ATG)
notes: ATG Nordic hamstring curl — feet anchored, lower body toward floor under eccentric hamstring control. Highest-evidence exercise for hamstring strain prevention and rehab. Begin with hands available to catch descent. Progress to full unassisted lowering. Contraindicated with acute proximal hamstring tendinopathy.

name: Reverse Nordic Curl (ATG Terminal Knee Extension)
notes: ATG reverse Nordic curl — kneel on soft surface, lean body backward under eccentric quad control. Directly targets rectus femoris and VMO through full knee extension range. ATG approach to terminal knee extension and patellofemoral rehab. Requires adequate knee flexion tolerance. Begin with partial range and progress to full lean.

name: Sandbag Deadlift
notes: Foundational sandbag pattern. Bag sits on floor; grip around middle or handles. Unstable load demands strong bracing compared to barbell equivalent.

name: Sandbag Clean
notes: Power clean mechanics applied to sandbag. Clean to shoulder or front rack position depending on bag style. Unstable load requires strong lat engagement at pull initiation.

name: Sandbag Clean and Press
notes: Core Horsemen compound — clean to shoulder then strict or push press overhead. Used in complexes and EMOM density work. Contraindicated with shoulder and wrist injuries due to overhead demand under unstable load.

name: Sandbag Back Squat (shoulder carry)
notes: Bag draped over one or both shoulders (bearhug or shoulder carry). Asymmetric loading variant challenges lateral bracing. Count toward durability due to non-standard carry position.

name: Sandbag Front Squat
notes: Bag held in front rack / bearhug position. Demands strong thoracic extension and anterior bracing. Appears in Horsemen strength days and named complexes.

name: Sandbag Bent-Over Row
notes: Hinge to roughly 45-degree torso angle; pull bag to lower chest. Unstable bag challenges grip and lat engagement throughout.

name: Sandbag Shoulder Carry (single shoulder)
notes: Bag loaded on one shoulder for distance or time. Heavily asymmetric — trains lateral bracing and single-side stability. Alternate shoulders each set or each round.

name: Sandbag Bearhug Carry
notes: Bag held tight against chest, arms wrapped around. Compresses anterior chain; excellent for work capacity circuits. Used in Horsemen GPP carries and loaded intervals.

name: Sandbag Get-Up
notes: TGU mechanics with sandbag on shoulder instead of kettlebell overhead. Used in Horsemen warm-ups and SMMF challenges (100 get-ups at 85-95#). Asymmetric load is more forgiving than overhead KB but demands robust bracing. Cited volume — 20 reps each shoulder at 80# — is a standard warm-up block.

name: Sandbag Lunge
notes: Walking lunge with bag on shoulder or in bearhug. Appears in Horsemen power endurance work ("Counting Dead Bodies") and SMMF 500m lunge challenge. Alternate carry position every 50m in long-distance variants.

name: Sandbag Half Moon
notes: From Horsemen notes — 'reach over body, pick up, arc high overhead, place down other side.' Heavy rotational ballistic. Strong bracing absolutely required throughout arc. Contraindicated with lumbar disc pathology due to combination of flexion-rotation under load.

name: Sandbag Squat Jump
notes: Explosive jump from squat position while holding sandbag in bearhug or shoulder carry. Appears in Horsemen power work and leg blaster variants. Land soft; prioritize triple extension at peak.

name: Sandbag Slam
notes: Lift bag overhead and throw/slam it down with maximal force. Unlike medicine ball slams, the bag doesn't bounce — requires picking it back up, adding a hip hinge rep per slam. Metabolically demanding. Used in Tabata Hell (Horsemen) and conditioning circuits.

name: Sandbag Overhead Press
notes: Strict press from shoulder or front rack position. Unstable bag demands more shoulder stabilizer activation than barbell. Can be pressed from one shoulder for unilateral challenge.

name: Horsemen Sandbag Complex
notes: Named Horsemen complex — multiple sandbag movements chained without rest. Example sequence: clean to shoulder → 5 deep squats → carry 40ft → drag back (ref: Double Eagle). Other variants chain clean + press + lunge. Used in EMOM format: 2× clean & squat + 3 burpees every minute for 20 min ('Teabag Your Buddy Tonight'). Treat reps as complex rounds — one rep = full sequence.

name: Basic Roll (Corta Capim / Role)
notes: Ido Portal corta capim / role work — continuous rolling patterns on the floor. Entry to all floreio ground work. 2-5 min continuous practice with 30-sec rest. Advanced variants include improvisation of learned movements. Contraindicated with cervical spine injury due to neck loading in rolls.

name: Au Cortado (Capoeira Cartwheel Variation)
notes: Capoeira-derived cartwheel-like movement — lateral hand-to-floor transition with inversion. Requires simultaneous bridge mobility and role foundation. Intermediate floreio element. Ido Portal programs 10-14 reps per set with 60-sec rest. Progression: Half Au Cortado → Au Cortado → Au Cortado Press.

name: Low Bridge (Rotations Into Low Bridge)
notes: Ground-level bridge from supine — low backbend with weight on hands and feet, hips low. Multiple difficulty levels of approach. Ido Portal programs 10 reps rotating into bridge. Slide into low bridge variant uses 5 reps × 5-sec hold. Foundation for all Floreio arch work.

name: High Bridge (Full Backbend)
notes: Full wheel / gymnastic bridge — hips driven high, arms and legs extended. Must master low bridge first. Static hold 30 sec (can be broken into attempts). Requires wrist, shoulder, thoracic, and hip flexor mobility simultaneously. Ido Portal programs rotations into high bridge (10 reps) and static high bridge hold (30 sec) as separate drill.

name: Bridge Walk (Front or Back)
notes: Locomotion in bridge position — front bridge walking (12m intermediate) or back bridge walking (15m advanced). Part of Ido Portal locomotion circuit programmed 3-5 rounds. Beginner variant is static hold 30 sec. Demands sustained spinal extension, shoulder stability, and hip flexor length under load.

name: QDR Rotational Push-Up (Quadruped)
notes: Ido Portal QDR (quadruped development) rotational push-up. Beginner 10-14 reps, 60-sec rest. Advanced variant lifts feet — 10 reps. Also programs QDR Circles (10 reps per side). Central element in Floreio Basic tri-set. Develops shoulder stability, scapular control, and thoracic rotation simultaneously.

name: Handstand Walk
notes: Freestanding handstand walking. Requires established freestanding handstand balance. Ido Portal locomotion circuit: beginner 12m (swinging to initiate), intermediate 12m, advanced 15m with elbow-to-knee variation. 3-5 rounds with 90-120-sec rest. Programs this as part of locomotion circuit with duck walk, horse walk, lizard walk, and ostrich walk.

name: Handstand (Freestanding)
notes: Freestanding handstand balance. Prerequisite to handstand walk. Ido Portal equilibre section: wall drill first (10-30 sec, 3-5 sets, 45-60 sec rest), then straighten into line (30-45 sec total, 2-3 sets, 90-sec rest). Handstand body line is primary quality — not duration.

name: Duck Walk
notes: Low squat walking. Ido Portal locomotion circuit — beginner and intermediate 24m, advanced 30m. Develops deep hip flexion, ankle dorsiflexion, and knee tolerance. Contraindicated with acute meniscus injury. One of five locomotion patterns programmed in circuit with 90-120-sec rest between rounds.

name: Horse Walk
notes: Quadruped locomotion with knees bent and floating above floor. Ido Portal locomotion circuit — beginner and intermediate 24m, advanced 30m. Develops hip mobility, shoulder stability, and proprioceptive coordination. Part of 3-5 round locomotion circuit.

name: Lizard Walk
notes: Low crawl pattern with hip and shoulder close to floor — derived from lizard movement. Ido Portal locomotion circuit: beginner 12m (beginner variant), intermediate 12m (advanced variant), advanced 15m. More shoulder-intensive than horse walk. Beginner and advanced variants differ in arm mechanics.

name: Ostrich Walk
notes: Hip-hinge-based walk — forward locomotion with torso pitched far forward and leg swing from hip. Ido Portal locomotion circuit — beginner and intermediate 24m, advanced 30m. Develops hamstring extensibility and hip hinge motor pattern under locomotor demand.

name: Frog Stance (Balance)
notes: Ido Portal equilibre entry — squat with knees resting on elbows, feet lifted, balance on hands. 15-30 sec per set, 3-5 sets, 60-sec rest. Builds wrist strength, shoulder stability, and proprioceptive feedback required for handstand progression. First step in the balance skill tree.

name: Air Baby Extensions (Ido Portal)
notes: Ido Portal equilibre movement — from ground support position, extend one leg laterally while counter-rotating upper body. Air Baby Extensions: 5-10 reps per side. Static Air Baby hold: 10-15 sec per side. Demands single-arm ground support strength, hip abductor mobility, and contralateral coordination.

name: Floreio Sequence (Combined Movement Flow)
notes: Ido Portal floreio — chained sequence of role, bridge, au cortado, and QDR movements without pause. Represents the integration tier (isolation → integration → spontaneous expression). Ido Portal programs as 2-min continuous role work with improvisation of learned elements, 3 sets, 30-sec rest. WOTD format uses descending pyramid (10/8/6 reps of sequence).

name: Cartwheel
notes: Standard cartwheel — prerequisite bridge between role work and au cortado. Establishes lateral inversion mechanics and hand-to-floor weight transfer. Both directions required. Progression toward au cortado once mechanics are clean and bilateral.
